1: wĕʾēlleh malkê hāʾāreṣ ʾašer hikkû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'these are the kings of the land that the Israelites struck.' The list form (king-roster) is a known genre in ANE victory texts; Assyrian and Egyptian annals feature comparable king-lists. Its function here is theological: the God who promised Abraham this land (Genesis 15:18-21) has delivered 31 rulers, beginning with the two trans-Jordan paradigm cases (Sihon and Og) established in Numbers 21.
2: Sihon king of the Amorites is first — his defeat (Numbers 21:21-31) was the breakthrough moment that silenced Israel's objection to crossing into Canaan. yōšēb bĕḥešbôn mōšēl mēʿărôʿēr — 'who lived in Heshbon, who ruled from Aroer.' The geographic precision (Arnon River, Jabbok River, half of Gilead) is an official boundary description, the language of land grants. The Mosaic victories function as the legal precedent for the Canaanite allotments.
3: The eastern Arabah — from Kinnereth (Sea of Galilee) to the Salt Sea (Dead Sea). ûmimmizraḥ haʿărābāh covers the full Jordan rift valley under Sihon's control. This eastern survey is the legal background for Joshua 13's trans-Jordan allotments; the boundaries here are not narrative decoration but the formal basis for tribal land titles.
4: Og of Bashan: min-yeter hārĕpāʾîm — 'one of the last of the Rephaim.' The Rephaim were the giant peoples of the pre-Israelite Canaan (Genesis 14:5; Joshua 15:20; Deuteronomy 2:20-21; Joshua 3:11). Og's famous iron bed (Deuteronomy 3:11) measured 9 cubits × 4 cubits — whether a bed or a sarcophagus, it was preserved as a monument to the victory. His territory reached to Hermon; the defeat of this last Rephaite king is the culmination of a campaign against supernatural-sized opponents that began in the wilderness.
5: Og's territory: Mount Hermon, Salecah, all of Bashan. The border with the Geshurites and Maacathites is significant: these peoples are not driven out (Joshua 13:13), and Geshur will later be David's maternal homeland (2 Samuel 3:3) — political geography and later narrative are already entangled in this boundary description.
6: mōšeh ʿebed YHWH ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl hikkûm — 'Moses the servant of the LORD and the Israelites struck them.' Sihon and Og are assigned to Moses; the trans-Jordan victories are Mosaic inheritance. wayyittĕnāh mōšeh yerûššāh — 'Moses gave it as a possession.' Yerûššāh (possession, inherited property) is legally stronger than mere conquest; the trans-Jordan tribes hold by Mosaic grant, not just military occupation.
7: The Cisjordan list begins: wĕʾēlleh malkê hāʾāreṣ ʾašer hikkāh yĕhôšuaʿ ûbĕnê yiśrāʾēl. The parallel structure (Mosaic victories / Josuaic victories) is deliberate: Joshua completes what Moses began. The geographic frame — Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley to Mount Halak in the south — defines the full conquest zone announced in ch.Joshua 1:4.
8: The land's varied terrain is listed: hill country, western foothills (šĕpēlāh), the Arabah, mountain slopes (ʾăšēdôt), wilderness, and Negev. These six zones correspond to the campaign zones of Joshua 10-11. The six peoples list (Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites) is the standard Deuteronomic enumeration (Deuteronomy 7:1) — the list signals that YHWH's mandate to dispossess these specific peoples has been carried out.
9: The Cisjordan list proper begins: king of Jericho (one), king of Ai (one). The formulaic melek ... ʾeḥād ('king of X, one') is the signature structure of the list. Starting with Jericho and Ai grounds the list in the paradigmatic first two victories of Joshua 6-8; every subsequent entry is measured against those standards.
10: Jerusalem and Hebron. Jerusalem's king Adonizedek was defeated in Joshua 10 but the city itself was not yet taken (Joshua 15:63; Judges 1:21 — it remains Jebusite until David). Hebron will become Caleb's inheritance (Joshua 14) and later David's first capital. The list includes the king as the defeated party; city occupation is a separate question.
11: Jarmuth, Lachish — part of the southern coalition defeated in Joshua 10. Lachish appears in later Assyrian records (the Lachish Reliefs of Sennacherib, ANET 287-288) as one of Judah's strongest fortresses; its prominence in the conquest list reflects its genuine strategic importance.
12: Eglon, Gezer. Gezer's king Horam was defeated (Joshua 10:33) but Gezer itself was not fully taken (Joshua 16:10: 'the Canaanites who lived in Gezer were not driven out'). The list records the king's defeat, not necessarily the city's complete occupation — a distinction the allotment chapters will clarify.
13: Debir, Geder. Debir's double appearance (here; ch.Joshua 15:15-17 as Othniel's conquest) reflects either a reconquest after Canaanite resettlement or variant traditions preserved side by side. The list does not attempt to resolve this; it records the Joshua-era defeat.
14: Hormah (ḥormāh, from ḥērem, 'devoted to destruction') — the name itself encodes the ḥērem ideology; its place-name likely commemorates an earlier battle (Numbers 14:45; Joshua 21:3). Arad — the Negev fortress whose king also attacked Israel in Numbers 21:1-3.
15: Libnah, Adullam — both in the Shephelah. Adullam will become famous as David's cave-refuge (1 Samuel 22:1), setting up a future narrative connection already embedded in the geography of conquest.
16: Makkedah, Bethel. Bethel's prominence in patriarchal narrative (Genesis 12:8; Joshua 28:19 — where Jacob named it 'house of God') gives its appearance in the conquest list theological weight: the place where YHWH promised Abraham and Jacob the land is now taken.
17: Tappuah, Hepher. Geographic completeness: the list is cataloguing the western foothills and northern Shephelah, ensuring no gap in the victory record.
18: Aphek, Lasharon. Aphek will be significant later: it is the Philistine staging ground when the ark is captured (1 Samuel 4:1) — conquest-era and monarchic-era narratives share geography.
19: Madon, Hazor. Hazor is the last of the major northern cities listed (the one Joshua burned, ch.Joshua 11:11); its appearance in this comprehensive list anchors the northern campaign's climax in the victory inventory.
20: Shimron-meron, Achshaph — northern Galilean cities of the Jabin coalition (ch.Joshua 11:1). Their inclusion confirms the list's geographic sweep: south to north, every major coalition city enumerated.
21: Taanach, Megiddo — the great northern fortress cities controlling the Jezreel Valley passes. Megiddo (later har Megiddon = Armageddon, Revelation 16:16) was not fully taken — Judges 1:27 notes Manasseh failed to drive out Taanach and Megiddo's Canaanites. The gap between the list's claim and later reality is intentional: the list asserts the kings' defeat, not permanent occupation.
22: Kedesh, Jokneam in Carmel — the Galilean hill country and the ridge leading to the coast. These are the northernmost cities in the Cisjordan list, completing the geographic arc from Jericho (Joshua 12:9) in the south to Galilee in the north.
23: Dor in the Naphoth-dor coastal plain, Goiim in Gilgal — filling out the Mediterranean coast. The inclusion of coastal cities alongside the inland cities marks the scope as truly comprehensive, even as subsequent chapters acknowledge Canaanite persistence in many of these areas.
24: kol-mĕlākîm šĕlōšîm wĕʾeḥād — 'all the kings, thirty-one.' The number closes the list with deliberate finality. Thirty-one defeated kings document total YHWH-given victory across every region of Canaan. The number itself is not mystically significant, but its precision as a closed inventory signals that the conquest narrative is complete: all that needed to be said about conquest has been said; what follows (Joshua 13-22) is inheritance distribution.
